title: Chatwoot vs Intercom for Solo users
meta_description: If you want less upkeep at work, Chatwoot fails first because ongoing self-hosting maintenance and infrastructure management. See why Intercom still holds up.
constraint_lens: You need a support tool that runs without ongoing upkeep, because you cannot spend time maintaining infrastructure or fixing issues.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for solo users who want less upkeep.
one_second_verdict.reason: Chatwoot fails first because it requires ongoing self-hosting maintenance and infrastructure management before running the system.

verdict.summary: Intercom is the better choice when you cannot afford to maintain your own system. It runs as a fully managed service, so updates, uptime, and infrastructure are handled for you. Chatwoot gives you ownership through self-hosting, but that control comes with ongoing maintenance tasks that quickly become a burden for a solo user.
verdict.decision_rule: If running the system requires ongoing self-hosting maintenance and infrastructure management, Chatwoot fails first.

sections.heading: Why Intercom fits this situation
sections.content: This setup fits a solo user who wants control over their support system but cannot spend time managing servers or fixing issues. Self-hosting sounds appealing, but it creates ongoing responsibilities. Intercom removes that burden by handling everything behind the scenes so you can focus on support instead of infrastructure.

bullets.point: Chatwoot can be self-hosted on your own server or infrastructure.
bullets.why_it_matters: You fully control your data and environment, but this requires setting up and maintaining the system yourself.

bullets.point: You manage updates, backups, and server performance directly.
bullets.why_it_matters: This gives flexibility, but creates ongoing work that a solo user must handle alone.

bullets.point: No dependency on a third-party service for hosting or uptime.
bullets.why_it_matters: This reduces reliance on external vendors, but shifts all responsibility onto you.

bullets.point: Intercom is fully hosted, with infrastructure, updates, and uptime managed automatically.
bullets.why_it_matters: You do not need to spend time maintaining servers or fixing issues, which keeps your workload low.

bullets.point: New features and fixes are deployed without manual intervention.
bullets.why_it_matters: This ensures the system stays up to date without requiring your attention.

bullets.point: System reliability is handled by the provider instead of the user.
bullets.why_it_matters: You avoid unexpected downtime or technical problems that would interrupt your support workflow.

sections.heading: How each tool can break down

items.fails_when: Chatwoot starts to break when you must handle server setup, updates, or downtime yourself while also managing support work.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Intercom when you want a system that runs without requiring ongoing maintenance.

items.fails_when: Intercom starts to break when you require full control over hosting and cannot rely on a third-party service.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Chatwoot when ownership of infrastructure is more important than reducing maintenance.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This verdict might flip if you have technical expertise or a managed hosting setup that removes most of the maintenance burden. In that case, Chatwoot can provide control without becoming a constant upkeep task.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick Intercom if you want zero maintenance and no infrastructure work.
Pick Chatwoot if you are willing to manage your own hosting.
If ongoing upkeep is a problem, choose Intercom.

faqs.q: Which tool better matches this priority?
faqs.a: Intercom fits this need better because Intercom is fully hosted, with infrastructure, updates, and uptime managed automatically. Chatwoot fails first when ongoing self-hosting maintenance and infrastructure management.

faqs.q: When should I choose Chatwoot instead?
faqs.a: Choose Chatwoot over Intercom when you require full control over hosting and cannot rely on a third-party service. Otherwise, Intercom remains the better fit for this comparison.

faqs.q: What makes Chatwoot fail first here?
faqs.a: Chatwoot fails first here when ongoing self-hosting maintenance and infrastructure management. That is the point where Intercom becomes the stronger pick.

faqs.q: Is this verdict only about one feature?
faqs.a: No. Intercom beats Chatwoot because Intercom is fully hosted, with infrastructure, updates, and uptime managed automatically, while Chatwoot loses once ongoing self-hosting maintenance and infrastructure management.
